title: Connecting Vision and Language
concept: CLIP connects computer vision and natural language processing by pre-training on pairs of images and text descriptions using contrastive learning.

exercises.prompt: Recap: What is the benefit of end-to-end fine-tuning for MAE models?

options.text: It allows the encoder weights to adapt to the target task, yielding higher accuracy.
It keeps the encoder weights completely locked.
It bypasses the need for validation.

exercises.explain: Correct! End-to-end fine-tuning lets the pre-trained encoder adapt to classification or detection features.

cards.title: Speaking of images
cards.body: For decades, vision models and language models lived in separate worlds. A vision model saw pixels, and a language model saw words. But humans connect them naturally: when you see a photo of a sunset, you can describe it in words. CLIP was built to bridge this gap, teaching AI to connect pixels to descriptions.

cards.title: Multimodal Learning
cards.body: CLIP (Contrastive Language-Image Pretraining) is a multimodal model developed by OpenAI. It is trained on 400 million image-text pairs from the internet to predict which caption matches which image, mapping both into a shared vector space.

cards.title: A bilingual dictionary
cards.body: Imagine learning a new language. Instead of memorizing words in isolation, you study a bilingual dictionary that links pictures directly to their written translations. By studying these pairs, you learn to connect visual objects to their names and descriptions simultaneously.

cards.title: Vector alignment
cards.body: If you feed CLIP an image of a golden retriever and the text sentence 'a fluffy golden retriever puppy playing in the grass', the model projects both inputs to vectors that sit extremely close to each other in its shared vector space.

cards.title: Not trained on class labels
cards.body: You might think CLIP was trained on standard classification folders (like cat vs. dog). In fact, CLIP is trained on raw, unstructured captions written by internet users, allowing it to learn a vast range of vocabulary beyond simple class lists.

left.text:
Processes only pixels
Output: Class IDs (e.g., 42)
Blind to natural language

right.text:
Aligns pixels & text vectors
Output: Shared vector coordinates
Can connect concepts to words

exercises.prompt: What is the core training objective of CLIP?
exercises.explain: CLIP uses contrastive learning to maximize similarity between matching image and text caption representations.

options.text: To align representation vectors of matching image-text pairs in a shared vector space
To translate French text descriptions into English
To segment the outline of objects using pixel masks

exercises.prompt: True or false: CLIP was trained on clean, human-curated ImageNet category folders.
exercises.explain: False. CLIP was pre-trained on a massive dataset of 400 million raw image-text pairs collected from the internet.
A shared vector space allows us to compare the distance between image vectors and text vectors directly.
exercises.sentence: CLIP maps images and text descriptions into a ___ vector space.

exercises.prompt: Which of these pairs represents a positive training match for CLIP?
exercises.explain: Matching captions and images form positive pairs, which CLIP tries to pull together during training.

options.text: An image of a cat paired with the caption 'A cute kitten sleeping on a couch'
An image of a car paired with the caption 'A blue whale in the ocean'
An empty black image paired with the caption 'A sunny day at the beach'

exercises.prompt: Why is pre-training on natural language captions superior to pre-training on a fixed set of classes?

options.text: Natural language captures a rich variety of relationships, actions, and attributes (like colors and styles) that simple class labels ignore.
Because text descriptions are much smaller than class folders.
Because language models are mathematically cheaper to train.
Because it forces the model to ignore spatial coordinates.

exercises.explain: Excellent! Captions provide rich semantic context, allowing the model to learn colors, numbers, actions, and styles. Next: the dual encoder architecture!
